volumeTitle: Children of Heracles. Hippolytus. Andromache. Hecuba
label: Andromache (Section 532 - Section 730)

sections.label: Section 532
sections.original: λείβομαι δάκρυσιν κόρας ,
sections.translation: Mine eyes are wet with tears , which trickle down my cheeks , as doth a sunless spring from a smooth rock . Ah me !

sections.label: Section 535
sections.original: ὤμοι μοι , τί δ ’ ἐγὼ κακῶν
sections.translation: What remedy , alas ! can I provide me ’ gainst my ills ?

sections.label: Section 537
sections.original: τί με προσπίτνεις , ἁλίαν πέτραν
sections.translation: Why fall at my knees in supplication ? hard as the rock and deaf as the wave am I . My own friends have I helped ,

sections.label: Section 540
sections.original: σοὶ δ ’ οὐδὲν ἔχω φίλτρον , ἐπεί τοι
sections.translation: but for thee have I no tie of affection ; for verily it cost me a great part of my life to capture Troy and thy mother ; so thou shalt reap the fruit thereof and into Hades ’ halls descend .

sections.label: Section 545
sections.original: καὶ μὴν δέδορκα τόνδε Πηλέα πέλας ,
sections.translation: Behold ! I see Peleus drawing nigh ; with aged step he hasteth hither .

sections.label: Section 547
sections.original: ὑμᾶς ἐρωτῶ τόν τ ’ ἐφεστῶτα σφαγῇ ,
sections.translation: calling out as he comes in sight . What means this ? I ask you and your executioner ; why is the palace in an uproar ? give a reason ; what mean your lawless machinations ?

sections.label: Section 550
sections.original: Μενέλα ’, ἐπίσχες · μὴ τάχυν ’ ἄνευ δίκης ⋯
sections.translation: Menelaus , hold thy hand . Seek not to outrun justice . To his attendant . Forward ! faster , faster ! for this matter , methinks , admits of no delay ; now if ever would I fain [*] resume the vigour of my youth . First however

sections.label: Section 555
sections.original: ἐμπνεύσομαι τῇδ ’· εἰπέ , τίνι δίκῃ χέρας
sections.translation: will I breathe new life into this captive , being to her as the breeze that blows a ship before the wind . Tell me , by what right have they pinioned thine arms and are dragging thee and thy child away ? like a ewe with her lamb art thou led to the slaughter , while I and thy lord were far away .

sections.label: Section 559
sections.original: οἵδ ’, ὦ γεραιέ , σὺν τέκνῳ θανουμένην
sections.translation: Behold them that are haling me and my child to death ,

sections.label: Section 560
sections.original: ἄγουσί μ ’ οὕτως ὡς ὁρᾷς . τί σοι λέγω ;
sections.translation: e ’ en as thou seest , aged prince . Why should I tell thee ? For not by one urgent summons alone but by countless messengers have I sent for thee . No doubt thou knowest by hearsay of the strife in this house with this man ’ s daughter , and the reason of my ruin .

sections.label: Section 565
sections.original: καὶ νῦν με βωμοῦ Θέτιδος , ἣ τὸν εὐγενῆ
sections.translation: So now they have torn and are dragging me from the altar of Thetis , the goddess of thy chiefest adoration and the mother of thy gallant son , without any proper trial , yea , and without waiting for my absent master ; because , forsooth , they knew my defencelessness

sections.label: Section 570
sections.original: γνόντες τέκνου τε τοῦδ ’, ὃν οὐδὲν αἴτιον
sections.translation: and my child ’ s , whom they mean to slay with me his hapless mother , though he has done no harm . But to thee , O sire , I make my supplication , prostrate at thy knees , though my hand cannot touch thy friendly beard ; save me , I adjure thee , reverend sir ,

sections.label: Section 575
sections.original: ῥῦσαί με πρὸς θεῶν · εἰ δὲ μή , θανούμεθα
sections.translation: or to thy shame and my sorrow shall we be slain .

sections.label: Section 577
sections.original: χαλᾶν κελεύω δεσμὰ πρὶν κλαίειν τινά ,
sections.translation: Loose her bonds , I say , ere some one rue it ; untie her folded hands .

sections.label: Section 579
sections.original: ἐγὼ δ ’ ἀπαυδῶ γ ’ ἄλλος οὐχ ἥσσων σέθεν
sections.translation: I forbid it , for besides [*] being a match for thee ,

sections.label: Section 580
sections.original: καὶ τῆσδε πολλῷ κυριώτερος γεγώς .
sections.translation: I have a far better right to her .

sections.label: Section 581
sections.original: πῶς ; ἦ τὸν ἀμὸν οἶκον οἰκήσεις μολὼν
sections.translation: What ! art thou come hither to set my house in order ? Art not content with ruling thy Spartans ?

sections.label: Section 583
sections.original: εἷλόν νιν αἰχμάλωτον ἐκ Τροίας ἐγώ .
sections.translation: She is my captive ; I took her from Troy .

sections.label: Section 584
sections.original: οὑμὸς δέ γ ’ αὐτὴν ἔλαβε παῖς παιδὸς γέρας .
sections.translation: Aye , but my son ’ s son received her as his prize .

sections.label: Section 585
sections.original: οὔκουν ἐκείνου τἀμὰ τἀκείνου τ ’ ἐμά ;
sections.translation: Is not all I have his , and all his mine ?

sections.label: Section 586
sections.original: ναί ,
sections.translation: For good , but not evil ends ; and surely not for murderous violence .

sections.label: Section 587
sections.original: ὡς τήνδ ’ ἀπάξεις οὔποτ ’ ἐξ ἐμῆς χερός .
sections.translation: Never shalt thou wrest her from my grasp .

sections.label: Section 588
sections.original: σκήπτρῳ δὲ τῷδε σὸν καθαιμάξω κάρα ;
sections.translation: With this good staff I ’ ll stain thy head with blood !

sections.label: Section 589
sections.original: ψαῦσόν γ ’, ἵν ’ εἰδῇς , καὶ πέλας πρόσελθέ μου .
sections.translation: Just touch me and see ! Approach one step !

sections.label: Section 590
sections.original: σὺ γὰρ μετ ’ ἀνδρῶν , ὦ κάκιστε κἀκ κακῶν ;
sections.translation: What ! shalt thou rank with men ? chief of cowards , son of cowards ! What right hast thou to any place ’ mongst men ? Thou who didst let a Phrygian rob thee of thy wife , leaving thy home without bolt or guard , [*] as if forsooth the cursed woman thou hadst there was a model of virtue .

sections.label: Section 595
sections.original: πασῶν κακίστην . οὐδ ’ ἂν εἰ βούλοιτό τις
sections.translation: No ! a Spartan ! maid could not be chaste , e ’ en if she would , who leaves her home and bares her limbs and lets her robe float free , to share ; with youths their races and their sports ,--

sections.label: Section 600
sections.original: κοινὰς ἔχουσι . κᾆτα θαυμάζειν χρεὼν
sections.translation: customs I cannot away with . Is it any wonder then that ye fail to educate your women in virtue ? Helen might have asked thee this , seeing that she said goodbye to thy affection and tripped off with her young gallant to a foreign land .

sections.label: Section 605
sections.original: κἄπειτ ’ ἐκείνης οὕνεχ ’ Ἑλλήνων ὄχλον
sections.translation: And yet for her sake thou didst marshal all the hosts of Hellas and lead them to Ilium , whereas thou shouldst have shown thy loathing for her by refusing to stir a spear , once thou hadst found her false ; yea , thou shouldst have let her stay there , and even paid a price to save ever having her back again .

sections.label: Section 610
sections.original: ἀλλ ’ οὔτι ταύτῃ σὸν φρόνημ ’ ἐπούρισας ,
sections.translation: But that was not at all the way thy thoughts were turned ; wherefore many a brave life hast thou ended , and many an aged mother hast thou left childless in her home , and grey-haired sires of gallant sons hast reft . Of that sad band am I a member , seeing in thee

sections.label: Section 615
sections.original: μιάστορ ’ ὥς τιν ’ εἰσδέδορκ ’ Ἀχιλλέως .
sections.translation: Achilles murderer like a malignant fiend ; for thou and thou alone hast returned from Troy without a scratch , bringing back thy splendid weapons in their splendid cases just as they went . As for me , I ever told that amorous boy to form no alliance with thee

sections.label: Section 620
sections.original: κῆδος ξυνάψαι μήτε δώμασιν λαβεῖν
sections.translation: nor take unto his home an evil mother ’ s child ; for daughters bear the marks of their mothers ’ ill-repute into their new homes . Wherefore , ye wooers , take heed to this my warning : Choose the daughter of a good mother . And more than this , with what wanton insult didst thou treat thy brother ,

sections.label: Section 625
sections.original: σφάξαι κελεύσας θυγατέρ ’ εὐηθέστατα ;
sections.translation: bidding him sacrifice his daughter in his simpleness ! So fearful wast thou of losing thy worthless wife . Then after capturing Troy ,– for thither too will I accompany thee ,– thou didst not slay that woman , when she was in thy power ; but as soon as thine eyes caught sight of her breast , thy sword was dropped

sections.label: Section 630
sections.original: φίλημ ’ ἐδέξω , προδότιν αἰκάλλων κύνα ,
sections.translation: and thou didst take her kisses , fondling the shameless traitress , too weak to stem thy hot desire , thou caitiff wretch ! Yet spite of all thou art the man to come and work havoc in my grandson ’ s halls when he is absent , seeking to slay with all indignity a poor weak woman and her babe ; but that babe

sections.label: Section 635
sections.original: καὶ τὴν ἐν οἴκοις σὴν καταστήσει κόρην ,
sections.translation: shall one day make thee and thy daughter in thy home rue it , e ’ en though his birth be trebly base . Yea , for oft ere now hath seed , sown on barren soil , prevailed o ’ er rich deep tilth , and many a bastard has proved a better man than children better born . Take thy daughter hence with thee ! Far better is it for mortals

sections.label: Section 640
sections.original: πένητα χρηστὸν ἢ κακὸν καὶ πλούσιον
sections.translation: to have a poor honest man either as married kin or friend than a wealthy knave ; but as for thee , thou art a thing of naught .

sections.label: Section 642
sections.original: σμικρᾶς ἀπ ’ ἀρχῆς νεῖκος ἀνθρώποις μέγα
sections.translation: The tongue from trifling causes contrives to breed great strife ’ mongst men ; wherefore are the wise most careful not to bring about a quarrel with their friends .

sections.label: Section 645
sections.original: τί δῆτ ’ ἂν εἴποις τοὺς γέροντας , ὡς σοφοί ,
sections.translation: Why , [*] pray , should one call these old men wise , or those who once had a reputation in Hellas for being so ? when thou , the great Peleus , son of a famous father , connected with me by marriage , [*] employest language disgraceful to thyself and abusive of me because of a barbarian woman ,

sections.label: Section 650
sections.original: ἣν χρῆν σ ’ ἐλαύνειν τήνδ ’ ὑπὲρ Νείλου ῥοὰς
sections.translation: though thou shouldst have banished her far [*] beyond the streams of Nile or Phasis , and ever encouraged me ; seeing that she comes from Asia ’ s continent where fell so many of the sons of Hellas , victims to the spear ; and likewise because she shared in the spilling of thy son ’ s blood ;

sections.label: Section 655
sections.original: Πάρις γάρ , ὃς σὸν παῖδ ’ ἔπεφν ’ Ἀχιλλέα ,
sections.translation: for [*] Paris who slew thy son Achilles , was brother to Hector , whose wife she was . And dost thou enter the same abode with her , and deign to let her share thy board , and suffer her to rear her brood of vipers in thy house ?

sections.label: Section 660
sections.original: ἁγὼ προνοίᾳ τῇ τε σῇ κἀμῇ , γέρον ,
sections.translation: But I , after all this foresight for thee , old man , and myself , am to have her torn from my clutches for wishing to slay her . Yet come now , for there is no disgrace in arguing the matter out ; suppose my daughter has no child , while this woman ’ s sons grow up , wilt thou set them up to rule the land of Phthia ,

sections.label: Section 665
sections.original: στήσεις τυράννους , βάρβαροι δ ’ ὄντες γένος
sections.translation: barbarians born and bred to lord it over Hellenes ? Am I then so void of sense because I hate injustice , and thou so full of cleverness ? Consider [*] yet another point ; say thou hadst given a daughter of thine to some citizen , and hadst then seen her thus treated ,

sections.label: Section 670
sections.original: σιγῇ καθῆσ ’ ἄν ; οὐ δοκῶ · ξένης δ ’ ὕπερ
sections.translation: wouldst thou have sat looking on in silence ? I trow not . Dost thou then for a foreigner rail thus at thy nearest friends ? Again , thou mayst say , husband and wife have an equally strong case if she is wronged by him , and similarly if he find her guilty of indiscretion in his house ;

sections.label: Section 675
sections.original: καὶ τῷ μὲν ἔστιν ἐν χεροῖν μέγα σθένος ,
sections.translation: yet while he has ample powers in his own hands , she depends on parents and friends for her case . Surely then I am right in helping my own kin ! Thou art in thy dotage ; for thou wilt do me more , good by speaking of my generalship than by concealing it .

sections.label: Section 680
sections.original: Ἑλένη δ ’ ἐμόχθησ ’ οὐχ ἑκοῦσ ’, ἀλλ ’ ἐκ θεῶν ,
sections.translation: Helen ’ s trouble was not of her own choosing , but sent by heaven , and it proved a great benefit to Hellas ; her sons , till then untried in war or arms , turned to deeds of prowess , and it is experience which teaches man all he knows .

sections.label: Section 685
sections.original: εἰ δ ’ εἰς πρόσοψιν τῆς ἐμῆς ἐλθὼν ἐγὼ
sections.translation: I showed my wisdom in refraining from slaying my wife , directly I caught sight of her . Would that thou too hadst ne ’ er slain Phocus ! [*]

sections.label: Section 688
sections.original: ταῦτ ’ εὖ φρονῶν σ ’ ἐπῆλθον , οὐκ ὀργῆς χάριν ·
sections.translation: All this I bring before thee in pure good-will , not from anger . But if thou resent it , thy tongue may wag till it ache ,

sections.label: Section 690
sections.original: μείζων , ἐμοὶ δὲ κέρδος ἡ προμηθία .
sections.translation: yet shall I gain by prudent forethought .

sections.label: Section 691
sections.original: παύσασθον ἤδη · λῷστα γὰρ μακρῷ τάδε ·
sections.translation: Cease now from idle words , ’ twere better far , for fear ye both alike go wrong .

sections.label: Section 693
sections.original: οἴμοι , καθ ’ Ἑλλάδ ’ ὡς κακῶς νομίζεται ·
sections.translation: Alas ! what evil customs now prevail in Hellas ! Whene ’ er the host sets up a trophy o ’ er the foe ,

sections.label: Section 695
sections.original: οὐ τῶν πονούντων τοὔργον ἡγοῦνται τόδε ,
sections.translation: men no more consider this the work of those who really toiled , but the general gets the credit for it . Now he was but one among ten thousand others to brandish his spear ; he only did the work of one ; but yet he wins more praise than they . Again , as magistrates in all the grandeur of office

sections.label: Section 700
sections.original: φρονοῦσι δήμου μεῖζον , ὄντες οὐδένες ·
sections.translation: they scorn the common folk , though they are naught themselves ; whereas those others are ten thousand times more wise than they , if daring combine with judgment . Even so thou and thy brother , now take your seats in all the swollen pride of Trojan fame and Trojan generalship ,

sections.label: Section 705
sections.original: μόχθοισιν ἄλλων καὶ πόνοις ἐπηρμένοι .
sections.translation: exalted by the toilsome efforts of others . [*] But I will teach thee henceforth to consider Idaean Paris a foe less terrible [*] than Peleus , unless forthwith thou pack from this roof , thou and thy childless daughter too , whom my own true son

sections.label: Section 710
sections.original: ἐλᾷ δι ’ οἴκων τῶνδ ’ ἐπισπάσας κόμης ·
sections.translation: will hale through his halls by the hair of her head ; for her barrenness will not let her endure fruitfulness in others , because she has no children herself . Still if she is unlucky in the matter of offspring , is that a reason why we should be left childless ?

sections.label: Section 715
sections.original: φθείρεσθε τῆσδε , δμῶες , ὡς ἂν ἐκμάθω
sections.translation: Begone ! ye varlets , let her go ! I will soon see if anyone will hinder me from loosing her hands .

sections.label: Section 717
sections.original: ἔπαιρε σαυτήν · ὡς ἐγὼ καίπερ τρέμων
sections.translation: To Andromache . Arise ; these trembling hands of mine will untie the twisted thongs that bind thee . Out on thee , coward ! is this how thou hast galled her wrists ?

sections.label: Section 720
sections.original: βοῦν ἢ λέοντ ’ ἤλπιζες ἐντείνειν βρόχοις ;
sections.translation: Didst think thou wert lashing up a lion or bull ? or wert afraid she would snatch a sword and defend herself against thee ? Come , child , nestle to thy mother ’ s arms ; help me loose her bonds ; I will yet rear thee in Phthia to be their bitter foe . If your reputation for prowess

sections.label: Section 725
sections.original: τοῖς Σπαρτιάταις δόξα καὶ μάχης ἀγών ,
sections.translation: and the battles ye have fought were taken from you Spartans , in all else , be very sure , you have not your inferiors .

sections.label: Section 727
sections.original: ἀνειμένον τι χρῆμα πρεσβυτῶν γένος
sections.translation: The race of old men practises no restraint ; and their testiness makes it hard to check them .

sections.label: Section 729
sections.original: ἄγαν προνωπὴς εἰς τὸ λοιδορεῖν φέρῃ ·
sections.translation: Thou art only too ready to rush into abuse ;

sections.label: Section 730
sections.original: ἐγὼ δὲ πρὸς βίαν μὲν εἰς Φθίαν μολὼν
sections.translation: while , as for me , I came to Phthia by constraint and have therefore no intention either of doing or suffering anything mean . Now must I return home , for I have no time to waste ; for there is a city not so very far from Sparta , which aforetime was friendly
